explicit_text: We moved to Oakland in the summer of 1903 after living in Calistoga. Mother bought a house at 531 - 20th Street, and we settled down there while all the boys started working. I worked various jobs in Oakland, including at the Oakland Cream Depot and later commuting to San Francisco. Oakland was growing, with places like Temescal, Fruitvale, and Dimond nearby, and it had a lively community with stores, parks, and transportation hubs like the Oakland Mole.
implicit_text: We moved to a bustling city in the East Bay in the summer of 1903 after living in a small town known for its vineyards. Mother bought a house at 531 - 20th Street, and we settled down there while all the boys started working. I worked various jobs in this city, including at a local cream depot and later commuting to a major nearby metropolis. The city was growing, with neighborhoods like Temescal, Fruitvale, and Dimond nearby, and it had a lively community with stores, parks, and transportation hubs like the ferry landing known as the Mole.

explicit_text: Before moving to Oakland, we lived in Calistoga where my father managed the Rochdale store. Everett worked with him there, keeping books and clerking, and ran the store after Father died in 1902 until a new manager was hired. Ralph and I helped out by picking prunes and grapes during school vacations, and Mother took in summer boarders to help support us. It was a tough time but we managed with help from the Rochdale directors and others.

explicit_text: My father was the manager of the Rochdale store in Calistoga before he died in January 1902. Everett worked there with him and took over running the store until a new manager was appointed. Later, Everett worked for the Rochdale Wholesale Co. in San Francisco and continued to buy food from the Rochdale store for the family. The directors of the Rochdale store helped us out during hard times after Father's death.

The directors of the cooperative store helped us out during hard times after Father's death.

explicit_text: I worked in San Francisco starting around June 1905 at Baker & Hamilton, crossing the bay daily from Oakland. The store was at Pine and Davis and Market Streets, a large four-story building with many departments. I also visited the Tivoli Theatre in San Francisco to see 'Floradora' and became familiar with the city’s lively streets and ferry landings. San Francisco was a bustling place with cobblestone streets and noisy drays, quite different from Oakland where I lived.

explicit_text: Ralph and I started attending Emerson School at 10th and Alice in Oakland, but only for a few days because we got jobs and had to work. School was short-lived for us as we needed to contribute to the family income. It was part of our early life in Oakland after we moved there in 1903.

explicit_text: I started working at Baker & Hamilton in San Francisco around June 1905, earning $15 a month. It was a well-established firm with a large four-story store where I learned about hardware and tools. My first job was as a weight clerk, and I worked my way through various departments. Everett helped me get the job, and it was an important step in my working life.

explicit_text: One day, Jack Schreiner, who lived next door, took me to the Tivoli Theatre in San Francisco to see 'Floradora.' It was the first show of that kind I ever saw, and it left a strong impression on me. The Tivoli was a notable place for entertainment in the city during those years.

explicit_text: The Key System in Oakland wasn't built until several years after I started working in San Francisco. Back then, people crossed the bay on ferries, and when the boats arrived at the Oakland Mole, there was a bustling scene with hotel runners and hack drivers competing for fares. The Key System came later and changed how people traveled around town.

The streetcar system came later and changed how people traveled around town.

explicit_text: The Oakland Mole was the ferry landing where crowds came off boats connecting with trains. It was a lively place with hotel runners and hack drivers competing for passengers, sometimes so fiercely that a cop had to keep order. I crossed the bay from Oakland Mole to San Francisco daily when I worked at Baker & Hamilton.

explicit_text: Henry, the nail slinger at Baker & Hamilton, was a little guy from the Bowery. He was incredibly strong and fast, throwing 106-pound kegs of nails all day. He took a liking to me and even started teaching me to box. The Bowery was known as a tough place, and Henry embodied that spirit.

explicit_text: We had some celebrities in Oakland like Jack London. I remember reading his stories, including 'Tales of the Fish Patrol,' and I recall his yacht, the Snark, moored at the foot of Broadway before he sailed to the South Pacific. Once, while delivering a suit for M.J. Keller, I went to his place at 30th and Grove, though I don't remember if I met him then.

explicit_text: I remember seeing Joaquin Miller, whose real name was Cincinnatus Heine Miller, on Washington Street near Keller's store. He had long hair, a big felt hat, and boots he wore all the time. Years later, I went with some young men from the First Methodist Church to visit him on Thanksgiving morning, and even found him in bed but ready to greet us.

explicit_text: Borax Smith made his fortune in the borax mines, specifically with Twenty Mule Team Borax, before moving to Oakland. He became involved in the streetcar and real estate business here. Jack London's novel 'Burning Daylight' was partly based on his life, which made him a notable figure in Oakland's history.

explicit_text: Temescal was a small town near Oakland when we arrived, located out at Telegraph Avenue and 50th Street.
